title: Selective staging and first commit

sources.claim: A commit stores an immutable snapshot reference, parent relationship, author/committer metadata, and message based on the current index, not every working-tree change.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A commit stores an immutable snapshot reference, parent relationship, author/committer metadata, and message based on the current index, not every working-tree change.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A learner stages one bug fix, reviews the staged diff, commits it with a causal message, and leaves an unrelated experiment unstaged. Your goal is to produce a commit whose staged diff matches its stated purpose. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Filesystem and terminal fluency; Working tree, index and HEAD; Ignore policy. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: A commit stores an immutable snapshot reference, parent relationship, author/committer metadata, and message based on the current index, not every working-tree change. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Amend and history rewriting create new commit objects, timestamps and identities can differ, and committing does not automatically publish to a remote. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Commit saves the entire project exactly as currently visible and sends it to GitHub in one operation.

includes: Stage an intentional patch and create one coherent snapshot.
Produce a commit whose staged diff matches its stated purpose.
excludes: Amend and history rewriting create new commit objects, timestamps and identities can differ, and committing does not automatically publish to a remote.

outcomes: Produce a commit whose staged diff matches its stated purpose.

examples.title: Selective staging and first commit: direct case
examples.situation: A learner stages one bug fix, reviews the staged diff, commits it with a causal message, and leaves an unrelated experiment unstaged.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A learner stages one bug fix, reviews the staged diff, commits it with a causal message, and leaves an unrelated experiment unstaged.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: A commit records the staged snapshot locally; inspect the index before committing and treat remote push as a separate authorized action.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because A commit records the staged snapshot locally; inspect the index before committing and treat remote push as a separate authorized action.

examples.title: Selective staging and first commit: boundary case
examples.situation: After committing, status still shows a modified local note because it was never added to the index.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: After committing, status still shows a modified local note because it was never added to the index.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Amend and history rewriting create new commit objects, timestamps and identities can differ, and committing does not automatically publish to a remote.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Commit saves the entire project exactly as currently visible and sends it to GitHub in one operation.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Produce a commit whose staged diff matches its stated purpose.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Commit saves the entire project exactly as currently visible and sends it to GitHub in one operation.
misconceptions.correction: A commit records the staged snapshot locally; inspect the index before committing and treat remote push as a separate authorized action.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Commit saves the entire project exactly as currently visible and sends it to GitHub in one operation.

analogy.example: A photo studio can model Selective staging and first commit: the set is the working tree, a selected proof is the index, and an archived photograph is a commit snapshot.
usefulFor: Separating working changes, staged content, committed snapshots, and repository metadata
limitations: Git stores content-addressed objects and references rather than physical photographs or one linear album.
Amend and history rewriting create new commit objects, timestamps and identities can differ, and committing does not automatically publish to a remote.

recap.summary: You should now be able to produce a commit whose staged diff matches its stated purpose. Keep this boundary in view: Amend and history rewriting create new commit objects, timestamps and identities can differ, and committing does not automatically publish to a remote.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Selective staging and first commit without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Commit saves the entire project exactly as currently visible and sends it to GitHub in one operation.
Apply the model to this case from memory: After committing, status still shows a modified local note because it was never added to the index.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Commit messages and scope”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
